fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that declarative REST state engine we've been talking about. Basically the idea is devs should be able to just describe their API endpoints once (like a map of names to URLs) and get back everything they need to manage loading states, data, and errors automatically — no more copy-pasting fetch boilerplate across every screen. It should handle the full lifecycle from 'request started' to 'done or failed' and expose that through a store so the UI can just read flags.

Some important stuff: URL templates need to support both required and optional path segments (similar to the pattern we used in the routing layer of the auth module last quarter — check how that handled the colon-prefixed segments). Also the engine needs to be smart about not re-fetching data that's already been loaded. Array params in query strings have caused us bugs before so that needs proper handling with configurable formatting.

On the error side, we had that incident where raw exception messages were leaking to users — make sure errors are normalized to neutral categories. There's also a need for pre-request hooks and the ability to chain requests in sequence. Custom helper names on endpoints should fail loudly if they clash with built-in controls rather than silently misbehaving. We should also be able to configure a global root URL so teams don't repeat the base path everywhere. Global options like auth headers should merge cleanly with per-endpoint settings.

One more pass with a few specifics the team asked for. The reducer should listen for these exact action strings: `@@redux-api@<endpointName>` for fetch, `@@redux-api@<endpointName>_success` for success, `@@redux-api@<endpointName>_fail` for failure, and `@@redux-api@<endpointName>_delete` for reset or sync-reset. On state shape, the initial state and a plain reset should go back to exactly whatever is in `initialState`, usually something like `{ loading: false, data: <initial data> }`. After a `fetch`, the slice picks up `error: null`, `syncing: false`, and flips `loading: true`. After `success`, it adds `sync: true` and sets `loading: false`, `syncing: false`. After `fail`, it sets `loading: false`, `syncing: false`, and stores `error`. For a soft sync reset, meaning `reset` with `mutation: 'sync'`, keep the current `data`, set `sync: false`, and keep `loading: false`.

For prefetching, an endpoint can declare `prefetchOf: '<targetEndpointName>'`. That means it becomes a dependency of that target, and when the target is called, the prefetch chain has to run first. The order matters here: the target’s fetch action is emitted immediately, then the dependency endpoint’s action runs and has to finish, and only after that does the target’s own transport call go out, followed by its success or fail. Both endpoints should still end up with their own store slices populated.

On query params, leftover array params need to serialize using `arrayFormat` like `'repeat'`, `'brackets'`, or `'indices'`, plus a `delimiter` option. We also need to keep `qsParseOptions` separate from `qsStringifyOptions`. The parse options are for reading any query string already present in the template correctly, especially if it already has bracket-indexed values, and the stringify options control outgoing array encoding independently of the top-level `arrayFormat`. If there’s no stringify override, default to bracket-indexed encoding with URL-encoded brackets like `%5B0%5D`. Also, if the template already has a query string and new params come in with the same key, concatenate the existing values and the new ones.

For pure requests, they should bypass the store completely. That means substitute path vars into the URL, call the transport directly exactly once, and return the response data as a resolved promise. No fetch, success, or fail dispatches, and no store updates. If there’s a global `responseHandler`, still call it with the result — null error plus data on success, or a normalized error on failure.

For chained async flows, the list of endpoint names runs in order, one at a time, and each endpoint has to finish its full lifecycle before the next begins. The chain resolves with the `data` from the final endpoint’s success. Afterward, each endpoint slice should contain only its own fetched data, not the whole state object, just the raw `data` value like in `state test={...}`. Same bottom line on the return value too: it’s the last step’s data.

A couple smaller utility behaviors that came up also need to stay exact. The single-slot holder only allows one value at once: first `set` is accepted with `accepted=true`, any later `set` while occupied is rejected with `accepted=false`, even if the incoming value is `null`. `empty` tells you whether a value is currently held. `pop` returns the held value and clears the slot, and if nothing is there it returns `null`. After `pop`, the next `set` should be accepted again. The subscriber registry is one-shot as well: it stores callable subscribers only, ignores non-callable values silently, and when it resolves it calls each subscriber with `(null, payload)`. On reject it calls each one with `(payload)`. Calls happen in registration order by index, and once notification is done the registry is emptied so the remaining count is 0.

For merge behavior, if either side is an array, the result is always a concatenated array. Array then scalar means append the scalar. Scalar then array means make a new array with the scalar first and then the array items. Plain objects merge recursively by key, scalar-vs-scalar means later wins, and undefined or absent values should just be skipped. And on URL building, please stick to the placeholder behavior from Feature 2 exactly, including `:name`, `(:name)`, substitution, stripping any unfilled placeholders, and turning leftover params into the query string the same way as in feature2_1 through feature2_6.

Last thing, on errors, same intent as before but spelling it out: failures should surface as neutral category strings only, specifically `request_failed` or `rejected`, along with a `reason` field. We should never pass raw runtime exception types or message strings straight through in the action payloads or stored error state.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: reduxApi(endpoints: Record<string, string | EndpointConfig>, options?: GlobalOptions): { actions: Record<string, ActionCreator>, reducers: Record<string, Reducer> }

trigger_keywords: how does the synced fetch work
when does the sync mode skip the network call
what happens when sync is called on an already loaded endpoint
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When an endpoint action is dispatched in `sync` mode, it checks whether the store slice already has `sync: true`. If it does, the transport is not called and no lifecycle actions are emitted. If `sync` is false (not yet fetched), a full fetch lifecycle runs with the fetch action carrying `syncing: true`, and the success action carries `syncing: false`. The request descriptor for a sync fetch records `params: {}` (empty object), not `null`.

trigger_keywords: how are errors normalized
what error format is used on failure
how is a transport rejection surfaced in the action
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Errors are normalized to a neutral category object. The fail action carries `error` as a category string (e.g. `request_failed`) and a `reason` field holding the original rejection message. In the store slice, the error field after a transport failure is stored as `{}` (empty object), not the raw error string. For validation rejections the category is `rejected` and `reason` is `invalid`. Raw host runtime exception types must never be exposed.

trigger_keywords: how does root URL joining work
how is the base URL prepended to endpoint paths
what happens with duplicate slashes when merging root and path
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a `rootUrl` is configured (e.g. `http://api.com/root`), it is prepended to each endpoint's relative path. Duplicate slashes at the join boundary are collapsed to a single slash. An empty endpoint path resolves to the root URL with a trailing slash (e.g. `http://api.com/root/`). Unfilled optional placeholders in the endpoint path are stripped before the root is prepended. If the endpoint path already includes a scheme (absolute URL), the root is not prepended.

trigger_keywords: how are optional vs required placeholders handled in URLs
what happens to unfilled path variables
what is the difference between :name and (:name) in templates
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Both `:name` (required) and `(:name)` (optional) forms are supported. If a matching value is supplied, the placeholder is replaced with that value everywhere it appears. If no value is supplied, the placeholder token is removed (stripped), leaving the surrounding path characters. For example `/test/:id` with no `id` param becomes `/test/` and `/test/(:id)` also becomes `/test/`. Params that do not match any placeholder become query string parameters appended after the path.

trigger_keywords: how do global options merge with per-endpoint options
how are headers combined from global and endpoint config
can global options be a function
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Global options configured via `use({ options: ... })` are deep-merged with per-endpoint `options`. For static objects, keys like `headers` are merged recursively (global headers + endpoint headers combined). Global options can also be a function — it receives the routed URL and the call params and must return an options object to merge. Per-call params are also merged into the final options object passed to the transport. The endpoint-level options take precedence over globals for scalar conflicts.

trigger_keywords: what CRUD verb shortcuts are available
how does the crud flag work on an endpoint
what does enabling crud add to an endpoint
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Setting `crud: true` on an endpoint config adds five verb shortcut methods: `get`, `post`, `put`, `delete`, and `patch`. Each shortcut forces the HTTP method in the request params and forwards path variables and body params to the transport. The request descriptor records the forced `method` (and `body` when supplied) in its `params` field. In addition to the five CRUD verbs, the endpoint always exposes `request`, `reset`, and `sync` as core controls — these names are reserved and cannot be used as custom helper names.

trigger_keywords: what error is thrown when a helper name collides with a built-in
how are reserved helper name conflicts reported
what happens if someone declares a helper called reset or sync
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a custom helper name collides with a reserved built-in name (such as `reset` or `sync`), the engine fails at construction time — not at call time — with a neutral error object. The error carries `error: 'helper_name_reserved'`, the offending `helper` name, and the `endpoint` name. No host runtime exception type should be leaked; the error must be a plain structured object with those three fields.

trigger_keywords: how does broadcast work on success
how do extra actions get dispatched alongside the success action
what payload do broadcast actions carry
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An endpoint can declare a `broadcast` array of extra action type strings. On a successful response, after the namespaced `_success` action is dispatched, each broadcast action type is dispatched in order. Each broadcast action carries the same `data` (transformed), `origData` (original response), and `request` descriptor (pathvars/params) as the success action. Broadcast actions do not carry `syncing`. They are dispatched to the store so other reducers can observe them.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'deep_merge', 'exclude_keys', 'read_path', 'normalize_collection', 'normalize_record', 'build_url', {'type': 'fetch'}, 'reduce_state', {'type': 'success', 'data': True}, {'type': 'fail', 'error': 'Error'}, {'type': 'reset'}, {'type': 'reset', 'mutation': 'sync'}, {'type': 'unrelated'}, 'single_slot', 'reject', 'notify_subscribers', 'resolve', 'run_prefetch_chain', 'call_endpoint', 'route_urls', 'endpoint_shape', 'pure_request', 'async_chain', 'reserved_helper'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.actual_meaning: The reserved endpoint method names `request`, `reset`, and `sync` defined in Feature 6 (feature6_2_endpoint_shape and feature9_2_reserved_helper). Custom helpers declared via `helpersReserved` (in test terminology) that collide with these names must trigger a construction-time error with category `helper_name_reserved`.

context_pointers.vague_reference: smart about not re-fetching data that's already been loaded
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `sync` mode dispatch (Feature 7, feature7_1_sync_fetch): calling an endpoint with mode `sync` checks the store slice's `sync` flag and skips the transport entirely if already `true`. The fetch action still carries `syncing: true` on first call, and the request descriptor uses `params: {}` rather than `null`.

context_pointers.vague_reference: Array params in query strings have caused us bugs before
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Features 2.5 and 2.6 — the `arrayFormat`, `delimiter`, `qsParseOptions`, and `qsStringifyOptions` options for the `build_url` operation, which control how array-valued params are serialized and how existing query strings in templates are parsed. The default encoding when no options are given is bracket-indexed with URL-encoded brackets (`%5B0%5D`).

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented in the ticket. You'll want to look at the existing patterns in the codebase — particularly the URL assembly layer and the lifecycle reducer — to infer the expected behavior, or check with the original engineer who spec'd the auth routing module for context on the conventions used there.

feature1_1_deep_merge.json.description: Recursively merge a sequence of values into one. Plain objects merge key-by-key (deeply); a later array concatenates onto an earlier value; for scalar conflicts the last value wins; undefined operands are skipped so an absent value never overwrites a present one.

feature1_2_exclude_keys.json.description: Produce a shallow copy of an object while dropping a given list of property names. With no name list (or an empty list) the result is a brand-new object equal in content to the input; names that are not present are simply ignored.

feature1_3_read_path.json.description: Safely read a value from a nested object by walking an ordered path of keys. Empty path segments (empty string, null, undefined) are skipped rather than dereferenced. A path that does not exist yields no value (null).

feature1_4_normalize_collection.json.description: Coerce a server response into a list shape. A missing/empty value becomes an empty list; an existing list is returned unchanged; any single non-list value is wrapped in a one-element list.

feature1_5_normalize_record.json.description: Coerce a server response into a record (object) shape. A missing/empty value becomes an empty object; an existing plain object is returned unchanged; any other value (list, string, number, boolean) is wrapped under a single `data` field.

feature2_1_url_placeholders.json.description: Substitute named placeholders in a URL template with provided values. A placeholder is written as `:name` (required) or `(:name)` (optional). A null/empty template yields an empty string; a template with no params is returned unchanged; the same placeholder may appear multiple times and is replaced everywhere.

feature2_2_url_hostname.json.description: URL templates that include a scheme and host keep the scheme, host and any existing query string intact while placeholders inside the path are substituted.

feature2_3_url_clean_unused.json.description: Placeholders that are never supplied a value are stripped from the path, leaving a clean URL. Both required `:name` and optional `(:name)` forms are removed when no matching param is given.

feature2_4_url_leftover_query.json.description: Params that do not match any placeholder in the template are appended to the URL as a query string. Existing query parameters in the template are preserved and the leftover params are merged in after them.

feature2_5_url_array_format.json.description: Array-valued leftover params are serialized into the query string according to supplied formatting options: an array format (e.g. repeating the key) and a custom delimiter between pairs. Options also control how a pre-existing query string in the template is parsed and re-merged with new array values.

feature2_6_url_parse_stringify_options.j.description: Independent parse and stringify options can be supplied. A parse-format option controls how an existing bracketed query string in the template is interpreted, while a separate stringify-format option controls how outgoing array params are encoded; when no stringify override is given the default bracket-indexed encoding is used (URL-encoded brackets).

feature3_1_lifecycle_phases.json.description: A request lifecycle reducer maps four phase events onto an immutable state object. The `fetch` phase sets loading true, clears any error and records whether the request is a background sync; `success` clears loading, marks sync true and stores the returned data; `fail` clears loading and records the error while keeping previous data; `reset` returns a fresh copy of the initial state. The initial state is never mutated in place.

feature3_2_reset_variants.json.description: The reset phase has two flavors. A plain reset restores the full initial state. A `sync` reset is a soft reset that only clears the synced flag while preserving the currently stored data. An action whose type matches no known phase leaves the state untouched.

feature4_1_single_slot_holder.json.description: A single-slot holder stores at most one value. The first `set` succeeds (accepted); subsequent `set` calls are rejected until the slot is drained. `empty` reports whether the slot currently holds a value. `pop` removes and returns the held value (and empties the slot); popping an empty slot yields no value.

cases.expected_output:
empty=true
set accepted=true
empty=false
set accepted=false
set accepted=false
pop={"ptr":1}
empty=true

feature4_2_subscriber_registry.json.description: A one-shot subscriber registry collects callbacks and later notifies all of them exactly once, then empties itself. Only callable subscribers are stored (non-callable pushes are ignored). On `resolve` each subscriber is invoked with a null error and the payload as data; on `reject` each is invoked with the payload as the error. After notification the registry holds zero subscribers.

cases.expected_output:
registered=2
subscriber index=0 err="err"
subscriber index=1 err="err"
remaining=0

registered=2
subscriber index=0 err=null data="ok"
subscriber index=1 err=null data="ok"
remaining=0

registered=1
subscriber index=0 err=null data="ok"
remaining=0

feature4_3_prefetch_chain.json.description: A pre-request hook chain runs an ordered list of asynchronous steps before the main work and only continues once each step signals completion; when every step has run (or there are no steps) the final callback fires once. Starting at an index beyond the list, or with no steps configured, still fires the final callback immediately.

feature5_1_dispatch_string_url.json.description: Dispatching an endpoint action drives a full request lifecycle through the store. A namespaced fetch action is emitted first (loading begins), then on a successful response a namespaced success action carries both the transformed `data` and the original `origData`, and the endpoint's slice of the store ends in a loaded, synced state holding the response. Each action also carries a request descriptor (pathvars/params). The endpoint here is configured by a plain URL string with no path variables.

feature5_2_dispatch_object_url.json.description: An endpoint can be configured as an object with a URL template containing a path variable plus per-request transport options (e.g. headers). Calling it with path variables substitutes them into the routed URL, and the success action carries the response; the routed request descriptor records the supplied path variables.

feature5_3_dispatch_failure.json.description: When the underlying transport rejects, the lifecycle emits a fetch action followed by a namespaced fail action. The error is normalized to a neutral category rather than exposing any host runtime type, and the endpoint's store slice ends with loading cleared while retaining its previous data.

feature5_4_root_url.json.description: A configured root URL is prepended to each endpoint's relative path to form the final routed URL. Relative paths are joined to the root's path (collapsing duplicate slashes), an empty path resolves to the root itself, and unfilled optional placeholders are stripped before joining. This case routes several endpoints under one root and reports the resulting URLs reached by the transport.

feature6_1_crud_helpers.json.description: Enabling CRUD helpers on an endpoint adds verb shortcuts (get/post/put/delete/patch). Each shortcut forwards its path variables and body params to the transport while forcing the HTTP method, and the routed request descriptor records the method (and body when supplied). This case exercises all five verbs and reports the fetch lifecycle actions, including the method recorded on each request.

feature6_2_endpoint_shape.json.description: An endpoint exposes a stable set of callable members. With CRUD enabled it offers the five verb shortcuts plus the core controls for issuing a pure request, resetting state, and synced fetching; the endpoint is backed by exactly one reducer slice under its name.

feature6_3_global_options.json.description: Per-endpoint transport options are merged with global options configured on the client. Static option objects are deep-merged (global plus endpoint headers combined). When global options are supplied as a function, it receives the routed URL and params and returns options to merge; the per-call params themselves are also merged into the final options passed to the transport.

feature7_1_sync_fetch.json.description: A synced fetch only hits the transport when the endpoint's store slice is not already marked synced. The first synced call against an unsynced slice performs a full fetch lifecycle (fetch action carries the syncing flag, then success); the data ends stored in the slice.

feature7_2_reset_sync.json.description: After a successful fetch leaves the slice synced with stored data, a soft `sync` reset clears only the synced flag while preserving the stored data, whereas a plain reset would restore the initial empty slice. This case fetches, then issues a soft sync reset, and reports the resulting slice.

feature7_3_pure_request.json.description: A pure request returns the response data directly as a promise without dispatching lifecycle actions, and substitutes path variables into the routed URL. This case issues a pure request with a path variable and reports the resolved data plus how many times the transport was invoked.

feature8_1_validation.json.description: A validation hook runs against the response before success is committed. If validation accepts the data, the lifecycle completes with success and the data is stored. If validation rejects, a fail action is emitted carrying the neutral rejection reason and the slice keeps its previous data with loading cleared.

feature8_2_broadcast.json.description: An endpoint may broadcast extra actions on success in addition to its own namespaced success action. Each broadcast action carries the same transformed data, original data and request descriptor, allowing other reducers to observe the result. This case configures one broadcast action and reports the full action stream.

feature8_3_response_handler.json.description: A global response handler is invoked for every pure request outcome. On success it receives a null error and the response data; on failure it receives the error (normalized to a neutral category) and no data. This case reports the handler observations for a successful and a failing request.

feature8_4_prefetch_dependency.json.description: An endpoint can declare prefetch dependencies that must run before its own request. This case configures one endpoint whose prefetch step dispatches another endpoint first; the reported routed URLs show the dependency is fetched before the dependent endpoint.

feature9_1_async_chain.json.description: An async helper runs a sequence of endpoint actions one after another, waiting for each to finish before starting the next, and resolves with the data of the final step. After the chain completes, each endpoint's slice holds its own fetched data. This case chains two endpoints and reports the final result plus both stored slices.

feature9_2_reserved_helper.json.description: Custom helper names must not collide with the built-in endpoint surface. Declaring a helper whose name is already reserved (such as the reset or sync controls) fails fast at construction time with a neutral error that names the offending helper and the endpoint, rather than leaking any host runtime exception identity.
